title: End of module: Complex Exponentials and Fourier Analysis

questions.text: Using Euler's formula e^{jθ} = cos θ + j sin θ, what is the real part of e^{j·(π/2)}?
questions.explanation: e^{j(π/2)} = cos(π/2) + j·sin(π/2) = 0 + j·1, so its real part is 0.

questions.text: Why does a real signal's spectrum always come in mirror-symmetric pairs (a component at +f matched by one at −f)?
choices: Because any real oscillation is built from complex exponentials in conjugate pairs, e.g. cos(ωt) = ½e^{jωt} + ½e^{-jωt}
Because all real signals have zero mean
Because negative frequencies aren't physically meaningful and are simply discarded
questions.explanation: Building a real-valued oscillation from complex exponentials always requires pairing a positive-frequency term with its complex conjugate at the corresponding negative frequency — this mirror symmetry is an unavoidable consequence of requiring the result to be real.

questions.text: A periodic signal repeats every T₀ = 0.04 s. What is its fundamental frequency f₀ = 1/T₀, in Hz?
The Fourier series coefficient formula c_k = (1/T₀)∫x(t)e^{-j2πkf₀t}dt is derived using which key property?
choices: Orthogonality: complex exponentials at different integer harmonics integrate to zero over one period, isolating a single term
The fact that all periodic signals are sinusoids
questions.explanation: Multiplying the Fourier series by e^{-j2πkf₀t} and integrating over one period kills every term except k (by orthogonality of complex exponentials at different harmonics), isolating c_k·T₀.

questions.text: For x(t) = 7 + 6cos(2πf₀t), what is c₁ (the coefficient of e^{j2πf₀t})?
The Fourier transform is best understood as:
choices: The continuous-frequency limit of the Fourier series, as the period T₀ grows without bound
A completely different tool unrelated to the Fourier series
A special case that only applies to signals with zero mean
questions.explanation: As T₀ → ∞, the fundamental frequency spacing f₀ = 1/T₀ shrinks to zero, and the discrete sum of harmonics becomes a continuous integral over frequency — the Fourier transform.

questions.text: Duality implies that a signal narrow in time will have a spectrum that is:
questions.explanation: You cannot simultaneously localize a signal tightly in both time and frequency — a narrow time-domain pulse (like a rectangle) requires a wide range of frequency components (like a sinc) to represent its sharp edges.

questions.text: Filtering a signal x(t) with impulse response h(t) gives y(t) = x(t)*h(t), so Y(f) = X(f)H(f). At some frequency, X(f) = 3 and H(f) = 4. What is Y(f) at that frequency?
questions.explanation: By the convolution theorem, convolution in time becomes multiplication in frequency: Y(f) = X(f)·H(f) = 3×4 = 12.

questions.text: A pure cosine at frequency f₀ transforms into:
choices: A single spike at f₀ only
A flat spectrum across all frequencies
questions.explanation: cos(ωt) = ½e^{jωt} + ½e^{-jωt} contributes one spike at +f₀ and one at −f₀ — the standard real-signal mirror-symmetry from earlier in this module.
